Executive Summary

  • ✅ Best staffing plan: Surge yields the lowest average wait time (46.6 min) with 58.1% ESI target compliance.
  • ❌ Mass-casualty impact: Normal → MCI increases wait from 45.0 to 83.0 min (Δ=38.1 min) and LWBS from 8.3% to 49.4%.
  • 📈 Wait–Satisfaction link: r = -0.787. Longer waits strongly erode patient satisfaction.
  • 🔗 Wait–LWBS correlation: r = 0.773. Reducing wait time directly reduces LWBS rates.
Jump to full analysis ↓

1. Design of Experiments

A full-factorial design explores how staffing levels, room configurations, and arrival patterns interact to shape Emergency Department performance.

Factors

FactorLevelsDescription
Staffing Plan4Minimal (3D/6N/1T), Standard (5D/10N/2T), Enhanced (7D/14N/3T), Surge (9D/18N/4T)
Room Config3Conservative (15S/5F/1T), Balanced (12S/8F/2T), FT-Heavy (10S/12F/2T)
Arrival Pattern3Normal (1×), Busy (1.5×), Mass-Casualty (2× + surge)

3. Monte Carlo Summary Dashboard

20-panel overview: distributions, heatmaps, main effects, Pareto front, time series, and queue dynamics.

3.1 Wait Time & Length of Stay

Patient wait time distributions by staffing plan and room configuration, length-of-stay profile, and left-without-being-seen rates.

3.2 Trade-offs & Patient Experience

How wait times relate to throughput and cost, door-to-doctor times by arrival pattern, and overall patient satisfaction.

3.3 Utilisation & Queue Dynamics

Resource utilisation heatmaps, wait-time interaction surfaces, ESI-level analysis, and 24-hour queue profiles.

3.4 Factor Effects & Pareto

Main-effect decomposition for wait time and LWBS, cost-vs-wait Pareto frontier, and target compliance metrics.

3.5 Temporal Patterns & Queue Peaks

Arrival vs discharge dynamics, doctor utilisation over 24 hours, and peak triage/treatment queue depths.

4. Key Insights

✅ Best staffing plan: Surge yields the lowest average wait time (46.6 min) with 58.1% ESI target compliance.
❌ Mass-casualty impact: Normal → MCI increases wait from 45.0 to 83.0 min (Δ=38.1 min) and LWBS from 8.3% to 49.4%.
📈 Wait–Satisfaction link: r = -0.787. Longer waits strongly erode patient satisfaction.
🔗 Wait–LWBS correlation: r = 0.773. Reducing wait time directly reduces LWBS rates.
💰 Cost trade-off: Cheapest staffing (Minimal) costs 1430€/pt but waits 85 min; best (Surge) costs 1336€/pt with 47 min wait.

9. Methodology & Model Description

9.1 Architecture

Discrete-event microsimulation with minute-by-minute time steps over a 48-hour period (first 24 h warm-up, second 24 h KPI collection). Self-contained simulation engine running inside ProcessPoolExecutor for embarrassingly parallel Monte Carlo execution.

9.2 Patient Model

Arrivals follow a time-varying Poisson process with hour-dependent rates (bimodal peaks at 10–14 h and 18–22 h). Each patient receives an ESI triage level (1–5) with defined probabilities, treatment durations, wait-time targets, and patience thresholds for LWBS behaviour.

9.3 Resource Model

Three resource types: Doctors (1 per treatment), Nurses (1 per triage + 1 per treatment), and Treatment Rooms (Standard for ESI 1–3, Fast-track for ESI 4–5, Trauma bays preferentially for ESI-1). Rooms undergo 15–25 min cleaning between patients.

9.4 Monte Carlo Design

4 staffing plans × 3 room configs × 3 arrival patterns = 36 combinations × 250 reps = 9000 runs. 48-hour (2 880-minute) simulation with 24 h warm-up each. Executed with 28 CPU cores in 39.8 seconds.

10. Interactive Analysis

11. Strategic Insights by Persona

Data-driven recommendations derived from simulation results, tailored for four key stakeholder groups.

01
💰
Cost Efficiency
Cost per patient ranges from 1270€ (Enhanced) to 1430€ (Minimal). Enhanced staffing offers the best cost-to-quality ratio.
02
📈
Throughput ROI
Surge processes 101 patients/24 h vs Minimal at 41. Each additional doctor adds ~10 patients/day.
03
🏆
Resource Utilisation
Doctor utilisation: 88% (Minimal) to 48% (Surge). Over-staffing reduces utilisation but improves patient outcomes.
04
🚨
Mass-Casualty Readiness
MCI scenarios generate 77 patients and push LWBS to 49.4%. Only Surge staffing maintains acceptable performance under MCI conditions.
05
⚡
Understaffing Risk
Minimal staffing shows wait times of 85 min and LWBS of 55.5%. Risk of patient harm and regulatory penalties is high.
01
⏱
Door-to-Doctor Performance
Average D2D time: 52.1 min (Surge) vs 92.9 min (Minimal). CMS benchmark is <30 min for ESI-2.
02
🎯
ESI Target Compliance
Overall compliance: 46.6%. ESI-1: 22.8%, ESI-2: 13.0%. Critical-acuity compliance must exceed 90%.
03
🚨
MCI Preparedness
Mass-casualty events increase wait by 38 min. The surge protocol with 9 doctors maintains D2D under 52 min even during MCI.
04
📊
Room Utilisation Gap
Room config matters: Conservative configuration minimises wait. Fast-track rooms handle 53% of patients (ESI 4–5) more efficiently.
05
🔬
Quality Metrics
Wait–LWBS correlation: r = 0.773. Every 10 min reduction in wait decreases LWBS by ~7.7%. Patient satisfaction peaks at 50.8 with Surge staffing.
01
📉
Nurse Utilisation Balance
Nurse util ranges from 27% to 53%. Optimal zone is 70–85%. Minimal exceeds safe thresholds.
02
⚖️
Triage Efficiency
Peak triage queue: 9 (Minimal) vs 1 (Surge). More triage stations dramatically reduce initial bottleneck.
03
🕐
Peak Demand Periods
Arrivals peak at hours 10–14 and 18–22 (up to 6 patients/h). Nurse staffing should be front-loaded to morning and evening shifts.
04
💪
Workload by Config
Nurses assist treatment at 1:2.5 ratio. With Surge plan (18 nurses), workload per nurse drops 2.0× compared to Minimal.
05
🔥
Burnout Risk
Nurse utilisation above 85% correlates with burnout. Minimal staffing puts nurses at 53% utilisation — unsustainable for sustained operations.
01
⏱
Wait Time Equity
ESI-1 waits: 44.4 min, ESI-5: 48.4 min. While triage prioritisation is appropriate, low-acuity patients face disproportionately long waits.
02
🚶
LWBS Impact
Overall LWBS rate: 26.1%. These patients may delay care, leading to worse outcomes. Surge staffing reduces LWBS to 7.4%.
03
💖
Satisfaction Drivers
Patient satisfaction: 35.7/100. Wait–satisfaction correlation: r = -0.787. Wait time is the single largest driver of patient experience.
04
🕔
Wait by Time of Day
Waits are longest during peak arrival hours (10–14 h, 18–22 h). Patients arriving at night (02–06 h) experience minimal delays.
05
🌟
Recommendations
Invest in Enhanced or Surge staffing, expand fast-track for low-acuity patients, and implement real-time wait-time displays to manage expectations and reduce LWBS.

12. Boarding Analysis

After treatment, admitted patients board in the ED while waiting for an inpatient bed. Boarding time depends on time of day (inpatient bed availability factors: overnight 0.3, morning 0.8, afternoon 0.6, evening 0.4). Higher factor = faster placement.

369 min
Avg Boarding Time
20
Avg Boarders / 24 h
69.7%
Boarding > 4 hrs
14:00
Peak Boarding Hour
25.3%
Overnight Boarding %
🚨 Boarding impact: ESI-1 patients board an average of 289 min, ESI-2: 362 min. 69.7% of boarders exceed the 4-hour threshold, indicating significant inpatient bed constraints overnight (factor 0.3).

14. Shift Handover Impact

At shift changes (07:00, 15:00, 23:00) a 15-minute handover period reduces treatment assignment throughput to 50%. This simulates the real-world impact of clinical handoffs, safety briefings, and staff transitions.

2
Avg Delayed Assignments / 24 h
3×15 min
Handover Windows
50%
Throughput During Handover
🕑 Handover insight: On average 2 treatment assignments are delayed per 24 h due to 3 shift handovers. Staggered handovers or dedicated handover teams could reduce this bottleneck by 30–50%, improving flow during peak transition hours.

15. Imaging & Lab Resource Utilisation

Discrete resource pools: CT scanners (2), X-ray machines (3), Lab stations (4). Patients queue for resources after triage before entering treatment.

25.9%
Imaging Utilisation
52.9%
Lab Utilisation
6.4 min
Avg Imaging Wait
🔬 Resource insight: Imaging utilisation at 25.9% and lab at 52.9%. Patients wait an average of 6.4 min for imaging. Consider adding a CT scanner if utilisation exceeds 80%.

🏥 KPI Glossary & Definitions

Detailed explanation of every metric used in this analysis, with industry benchmarks and authoritative sources to help all stakeholders interpret results.

KPIDefinitionWhy It MattersIndustry Benchmark
Avg Wait [min]Mean time from arrival to first treatmentPrimary patient experience metric. Drives LWBS and satisfaction.CMS benchmark: ≤28 min median
Door-to-Doctor [min]Time from arrival to first physician contactCMS quality metric (ED-1b). Strong predictor of outcomes for ESI 1–2.CMS: median ≤28 min
LOS [min]Total time from arrival to discharge/admitIncludes treatment + boarding. Boarding inflates LOS without improving care.Median: 120–180 min; >240 = concern
LWBS Rate [%]Patients leaving without being seenSafety risk: LWBS patients may have serious undiagnosed conditions.Target: <2% (ACEP)
ThroughputPatients treated per 24-hour periodDepartmental capacity metric. Limited by beds, staff, and boarding.Varies by ED size; compare vs. arrivals
Doctor Utilization [%]Fraction of time physicians are treatingBelow 60%: potential overstaffing. Above 90%: queueing delays.70–85% optimal (Green, 2006)
Nurse Utilization [%]Fraction of time nurses are in patient careNurses handle triage, vitals, treatment assist, and discharge tasks.65–80% recommended
Room Utilization [%]Fraction of time treatment rooms occupiedPhysical capacity constraint. Above 85%: hallway boarding starts.Bernstein et al., 2009
Target Compliance [%]Fraction meeting ESI-based time targetsRegulatory metric: ESI 1 ≤10 min, ESI 2 ≤15 min, etc.Target: ≥90% for ESI 1–2
SatisfactionComposite patient satisfaction score (0–100)Linked to HCAHPS scores and hospital reimbursement.Top quartile: >80
Cost/Patient [€]Total operating cost per patient encounterStaff + facility + supplies. Key for ED financial sustainability.National avg: $400–600 per visit

📚 Sources & References

Industry standards, academic research, and data sources backing the model assumptions and benchmarks cited in this report.

#SourceUsed For
1ACEP — American College of Emergency PhysiciansLWBS rate benchmarks, boarding guidelines, triage standards
2CMS — Emergency Department Quality ReportingDoor-to-Doctor (ED-1b), median wait time, and LOS quality measures
3Green, L. (2006). Operations ResearchQueueing models for ED staffing and capacity planning
4Welch, S. (2010). Annals of Emergency MedicineWait time perception and patient satisfaction relationship
5Bernstein et al. (2009). Ann. Emergency MedicineED crowding metrics and clinical impact of hallway boarding
6Handel et al. (2011). Academic Emergency MedicineSimulation-based ED staffing optimization and DES validation
